first_paragraph: [Swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") has been a sport at every modern [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics"). It has been open to women since [1912](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics"). At the Olympics, swimming has the second-highest number of medal-contested events after [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Athletics at the Summer Olympics").

[Swimming](/wiki/Swimming_(sport) "Swimming (sport)") has been a sport at every modern [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics"). It has been open to women since [1912](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics"). At the Olympics, swimming has the second-highest number of medal-contested events after [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Athletics at the Summer Olympics").

## Olympic records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swimming_at_the_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Olympic records")]

Main article: [List of Olympic records in swimming](/wiki/List_of_Olympic_records_in_swimming "List of Olympic records in swimming")

## Notable changes to the sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swimming_at_the_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Notable changes to the sport")]

Times have consistently dropped over the years due to better training techniques and new developments within the sport.

In the first four Olympics, competitions were not held in pools, but rather in open water (1896, the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"); 1900, the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine"); 1904, an artificial lake; 1906, the Mediterranean). The [1904 Olympics](/wiki/1904_Olympics "1904 Olympics")' races were the only ones ever measured in yards, instead of the usual metres. A 100-metre pool was built for the [1908 Olympics](/wiki/1908_Olympics "1908 Olympics") and was located in the centre of the main stadium's track and field oval. The [1912 Olympics](/wiki/1912_Olympics "1912 Olympics"), held in the Stockholm harbor, marked the beginning of electrical timing.

Male swimmers wore full body swimsuits up until the 1940s, which caused more drag in the water than their modern swim-wear counterparts. Also, over the years, pool designs have lessened the drag. Some design considerations allow for the reduction of swimming [resistance](/wiki/Drag_(physics) "Drag (physics)") making the pool faster, namely, proper pool depth, elimination of waves, elimination of currents, increased lane width, energy absorbing racing lane lines and gutters, and the use of other innovative hydraulic, acoustic, illumination, and swimwear designs.

The [1924 Olympics](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics") were the first to use the standard 50-metre pool with marked lanes (a standard that remains to this day). In the freestyle, swimmers originally dove from the pool walls, but diving blocks were first incorporated at the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics"). The [flip-turn](/wiki/Flip-turn "Flip-turn") was developed by the 1950s. [Swimming goggles](/wiki/Swedish_goggles "Swedish goggles") were first allowed in [1976](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics").

The [butterfly stroke](/wiki/Butterfly_stroke "Butterfly stroke") events were not held until [1956](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics"). Previous rules permitted the butterfly stroke in [breaststroke](/wiki/Breaststroke "Breaststroke") races. After 1956, when these rules were changed, butterfly became its own stroke entirely.

The 800 m event was added to the women's programme in [1968](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics"); whilst longer, this event was not as far as the men's 1500 m event. This discrepancy was evened out in 2020: that year, the 1500 metres was included in the women's programme for the first time and the 800 metres freestyle was added to the men's programme.

Both men and women were granted the 200 metres freestyle race in [1968](/wiki/1968_Summer_Olympics "1968 Summer Olympics"), giving swimmers an intermediate distance race between 100 metres and 400 metres.

The women's 4 **×** 200 metres freestyle relay race was added in [1996](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1996_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_4_%C3%97_200_metre_freestyle_relay "Swimming at the 1996 Summer Olympics – Women's 4 × 200 metre freestyle relay")—a men's relay race at that distance had been held since [1912](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1912_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_200_metre_freestyle_relay "Swimming at the 1912 Summer Olympics – Men's 4 × 200 metre freestyle relay"); interestingly, the winning time for the women's 4 **×** 200 metres freestyle relay in 1996 was more than two minutes faster than the men's 4 **×** 200 metres freestyle relay of 1912. At six Games, the men had two freestyle relay races, while the women had one. Starting with [1996](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics"), both genders have two.

The medley relay races (4 **×** 100 metres) were not held for men or for women until 1960, but they have continued in every Games since then.

Starting in [2008](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), both men and women compete in 10 km [open water](/wiki/Open_water_swimming "Open water swimming") marathon swim events.

The mixed 4 **×** 100 metres medley relay (2 women and 2 men) was added to the programme in the [2020 Olympic Games](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").

the men’s and women’s 50 m backstroke, breaststroke, and butterfly were added to the programme in the [2028 Olympic Games](/wiki/2028_Summer_Olympics "2028 Summer Olympics").

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Swimming_at_the_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-1904Games_1-8) Distances at the 1904 Olympics were measured in yards rather than metres. The 50 yard freestyle was 45.7 metres and the 100 yard freestyle and backstroke races were 91.4 metres. The 220, 440, and 880 yard freestyle races were 201, 402, and 805 metres, respectively. Instead of a 1500 metre race, there was a 1 mile race which made for 1609 metres. The 440 yard breaststroke, like the freestyle, was 402 metres. The relay, 4 × 50 yards, was 4 × 45.7 metres.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-W300_2-0)** In 1920, the women's event was 300 metres.
